reasoning: The question evaluates whether at least one of Q1, Q2, or Q3 2026 experiences negative GDP growth according to the BEA's third estimate. Although baseline real GDP forecasts for the US in 2026 are mostly optimistic (around 2.5% to 2.8% for the full year by Goldman Sachs and Oxford Economics), structural vulnerabilities persist. Foremost, growth momentum significantly slowed to an annualized rate of 0.5% in Q4 2025. Additionally, the recent macro shocks, including geopolitical instability (the "Iran war" and rising oil prices), alongside softening labor market expectations, introduce substantial downside risk. Given that the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow model recently lowered its Q1 2026 tracker to 1.3%, Q1 remains in positive territory but close enough to zero that a moderate error or unexpected inventory drag could push it negative (the model has historically had an RMSE of 1.17 percentage points). Polymarket and major banks estimate the risk of an outright recession in 2026 at approximately 25-31%, suggesting the probability of at least one isolated negative quarter in the first three quarters of the year is moderately higher — likely around 35-40%. Thus, my forecast centers around 0.36, with slightly higher uncertainty for longer horizons.
